metadata.description: Trait 系统: Traits define shared behavior. Generic constraint `fn foo<T: Display>(t: T)`. Trait objects. Here's a brief explanation with a Python code example to illustrate the concept: In Rust, traits are used to define shared behavior that can be implemented by multiple types. A generic function can constrain its parameters to implement a specific trait, ensuring that only types capable of fulfilling the trait's requirements can be passed as arguments. For instance, in Rust: ```rust trait Display { fn fmt(&self, f: &mut std::fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> std::fmt::Result; } fn foo<T: Display>(t: T) { // The function `foo` requires its parameter to implement the `Display` trait. println!("Displaying value: {:?}", t); } ``` In Python, while there's no direct equivalent of Rust traits, you can achieve similar functionality using abstract base classes (ABCs) from the `abc` module. Here’s a simple example: ```python from abc import ABC, abstractmethod class Display(ABC): @abstractmethod def display(self): pass def foo(t: Display): # The function `foo` requires its parameter to implement the `Display` interface. print("Displaying value:", t.display()) # Example implementation of the Display trait class MyDisplay(Display): def display(self): return "Hello, World!" foo(MyDisplay()) # Output: Displaying value: Hello, World! ``` This Python example uses an abstract base class `Display` with a method `display`, and a generic function `foo` that requires its argument to implement this interface.
